request.question: What was the name of Red Hot Chili Peppers 1989 album released by EMI Records?

title: Silverlake Conservatory of Music
Red Hot Chili Peppers 1983 Tour
Red Hot Chili Peppers 1984 Tour
Good Time Boys
The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers
John Frusciante discography
Ataxia (band)
Red Hot Skate Rock
The Red Hot Chili Peppers (album)
Mother's Milk
sentences: Silverlake Conservatory of Music is a nonprofit educational organization formed in California.

It was founded in 2001 by Red Hot Chili Peppers bassist, Michael "Flea" Balzary and Keith "Tree" Barry (longtime touring musician with the Chili Peppers) to foster music education, with Chili Pepper's member Anthony Kiedis also on the board.

The facility organizes an annual "Hullabaloo", one of which featured performances by Red Hot Chili Peppers, Eddie Vedder and Charlie Haden, and the Chili Peppers raised $1 million for the Conservatory during an August 24, 2011 performance at Club Nokia.
The Red Hot Chili Peppers 1983 Tour was the first-ever concert tour by Red Hot Chili Peppers.
Originally formed as a joke band called "Tony Flow And The Miraculously Majestic Masters Of Mayhem" by bassist Flea, guitarist, Hillel Slovak and drummer, Jack Irons, all friends from high school.
They asked fellow high school friend, Anthony Kiedis to join them onstage for their first-ever show at the Hollywood, California venue known as The Rhythm Lounge.
Kiedis had previously been a MC to introduce his friend's former bands.
However, this time Kiedis was asked to sing.
With one song, a made-up rap Kiedis created called "Out In L.A.", the band took to the stage as an opening act for Neighbor's Voices.
The club owner was so impressed by their performance he told them to return the following week with another song.
The band quickly wrote "Get Up and Jump" and played it at that second show.
In early March 1983 the band made the name change to the Red Hot Chili Peppers.

The band first made a name for themselves on their local club scene when they played the famous Kit Kat Club by doing their now famous socks on cocks routine (this would become a trademark for the band and something they would do on occasion for many years).
After this performance, Lindy Goetz decided to be their manager, a role he would hold until 1999.
The 1983 tour consisted of shows mostly in the band's homestate of California except for one show in Nevada.
A 10-song demo tape was recorded with Spit Stix, drummer for the punk band Fear, which Flea was briefly with.
Red Hot Chili Peppers 1984 Tour was a concert tour by Red Hot Chili Peppers to support their 1984 debut album, "The Red Hot Chili Peppers".
Following the band's brief first tour the previous year, original members, Hillel Slovak and Jack Irons left the band to tour and record with their own band, What Is This?
who had also obtained a record contract shortly before the Chili Peppers did.
Slovak and Irons considered the Chili Peppers a side project and What Is This?
to have the brighter future.
In late 1983, auditions were held and Irons was quickly replaced by Cliff Martinez, best known for his work with The Weirdos, The Dickies and Captain Beefheart.
Dix Denney, Martinez's Weirdo's bandmate was close to becoming the band's new guitarist however after a few rehearsals they felt Denney's style didn't match what the band wanted.
Through further auditions it came down to a guy named Mark Nine and Jack Sherman.
The band had no clue as to who Sherman was or how he got to the audition but knew he was the best fit because he instantly clicked musically with bassist Flea and Martinez.
"Good Time Boys" is a song by the Red Hot Chili Peppers and the first song from their 1989 album, "Mother's Milk".
The song contains excerpts of "Bonin' in the Boneyard" by Fishbone, "Try" by Thelonious Monster and "White Girl" by X.
The song is also notable for including backing vocals by the band's former guitarist, Jack Sherman who was fired in 1984.

The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers (also titled Greatest Hits on some versions) is a compilation album by the Red Hot Chili Peppers, first released in 1994, containing tracks from the band's time signed to the EMI America record label. "
What Hits!?
" has all these songs and 8 more.
This compilation has been reprinted at least 4 times from 1994 to 2005 with different cover art.

The discography of John Frusciante, who is best known as the former guitarist of the rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, consists of eleven solo albums and four EPs, as well as two albums with collaborators Joe Lally and Josh Klinghoffer under the name Ataxia and one EP and one album under the alias of Trickfinger.
Frusciante left the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1992 after he got tired of the growing success of the band.
He released his first solo album, "Niandra LaDes and Usually Just a T-Shirt", in 1994 on American Recordings.
His second record, "Smile From the Streets You Hold", was released in 1997 and later taken off the market at his request in 1999.

After returning to the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1998, Frusciante recorded "Californication" with the band and subsequently released his third solo album, "To Record Only Water for Ten Days", in February 2001 on Warner Music Group.

Ataxia was a short-lived American experimental rock band formed in 2004 by guitarist John Frusciante (Red Hot Chili Peppers), bassist Joe Lally (Fugazi) and drummer Josh Klinghoffer (Red Hot Chili Peppers, Dot Hacker, The Bicycle Thief), who later succeeded Frusciante as the lead guitarist of the Red Hot Chili Peppers.
Red Hot Skate Rock is a 30-minute music film filmed on September 20, 1987 by Vision Street Wear at the Vision Skate Escape in Los Angeles.

The film features an 8-song performance by the Red Hot Chili Peppers during the band's The Uplift Mofo Party Plan tour and includes skate demos by skateboarders Tony Hawk, Steve Caballero, Chris Miller and many more pro skaters.
To date, Red Hot Skate Rock was the first and only officially released video recording of the original Red Hot Chili Peppers lineup.
Guitarist Hillel Slovak died of a drug overdose less than a year later and Irons would quit shortly after Slovak's death.
The Red Hot Chili Peppers is the debut studio album by American rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, released on August 10, 1984 on EMI Records.
The album was produced by Gang of Four guitarist Andy Gill, and is the only album to feature Jack Sherman on guitar.
Sherman was fired by the band after the album release tour, replaced by founding member Hillel Slovak. "
Get Up and Jump" was the only single released from the album, but a music video was made for the song "True Men Don't Kill Coyotes".
Mother's Milk is the fourth studio album by American funk rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, released on August 16, 1989, by EMI Records.

After the death of founding guitarist Hillel Slovak and subsequent departure of drummer Jack Irons, vocalist Anthony Kiedis and bassist Flea regrouped with the addition of guitarist John Frusciante and drummer Chad Smith.
Frusciante's influence altered the band's sound by placing more emphasis on melody than rhythm, which had dominated the band's previous material.

Returning producer Michael Beinhorn favored heavy metal guitar riffs as well as overdubbing that was perceived by Frusciante as excessive, and as a result Beinhorn and Frusciante constantly fought over the album's guitar sound.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What was the name of Red Hot Chili Peppers 1989 album released by EMI Records?
hop1_docs: The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers | The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers (also titled Greatest Hits on some versions) is a compilation album by the Red Hot Chili Peppers, first released in 1994, containing tracks from the band's time signed to the EMI America record label. " What Hits!? " has all these songs and 8 more.  This compilation has been reprinted at least 4 times from 1994 to 2005 with different cover art.

The Red Hot Chili Peppers (album) | The Red Hot Chili Peppers is the debut studio album by American rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, released on August 10, 1984 on EMI Records.  The album was produced by Gang of Four guitarist Andy Gill, and is the only album to feature Jack Sherman on guitar.  Sherman was fired by the band after the album release tour, replaced by founding member Hillel Slovak. " Get Up and Jump" was the only single released from the album, but a music video was made for the song "True Men Don't Kill Coyotes".

Sock-Cess | Sock-Cess is a 1989 compilation album by Red Hot Chili Peppers and was released exclusively in the UK by EMI as a promotional only release.  The album contains tracks from the band's first four studio albums.

Red Hot Chili Peppers 1984 Tour | Red Hot Chili Peppers 1984 Tour was a concert tour by Red Hot Chili Peppers to support their 1984 debut album, "The Red Hot Chili Peppers".  Following the band's brief first tour the previous year, original members, Hillel Slovak and Jack Irons left the band to tour and record with their own band, What Is This?  who had also obtained a record contract shortly before the Chili Peppers did.  Slovak and Irons considered the Chili Peppers a side project and What Is This?  to have the brighter future.  In late 1983, auditions were held and Irons was quickly replaced by Cliff Martinez, best known for his work with The Weirdos, The Dickies and Captain Beefheart.  Dix Denney, Martinez's Weirdo's bandmate was close to becoming the band's new guitarist however after a few rehearsals they felt Denney's style didn't match what the band wanted.  Through further auditions it came down to a guy named Mark Nine and Jack Sherman.  The band had no clue as to who Sherman was or how he got to the audition but knew he was the best fit because he instantly clicked musically with bassist Flea and Martinez.

Woodstock 1994 (Red Hot Chili Peppers album) | Woodstock 1994 is a bootleg recording and a "unofficial release" of the Red Hot Chili Peppers, recorded on August 14, 1994 at Woodstock '94.  The concert was released on CD and 2-LP Vinyl in 2016.

Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers | Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers is a compilation album released on March 31, 1998 by the Red Hot Chili Peppers.  All of the songs featured are covers mainly from the band's early albums.

John Frusciante discography | The discography of John Frusciante, who is best known as the former guitarist of the rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, consists of eleven solo albums and four EPs, as well as two albums with collaborators Joe Lally and Josh Klinghoffer under the name Ataxia and one EP and one album under the alias of Trickfinger.  Frusciante left the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1992 after he got tired of the growing success of the band.  He released his first solo album, "Niandra LaDes and Usually Just a T-Shirt", in 1994 on American Recordings.  His second record, "Smile From the Streets You Hold", was released in 1997 and later taken off the market at his request in 1999.  After returning to the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 1998, Frusciante recorded "Californication" with the band and subsequently released his third solo album, "To Record Only Water for Ten Days", in February 2001 on Warner Music Group.
hop1_titles: The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers
Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Sock-Cess — [3] "Sock-Cess is a 1989 compilation album"
- Sock-Cess — [3] "released exclusively in the UK by EMI"
- The Red Hot Chili Peppers (album) — [2] "released on August 10, 1984 on EMI Records."
- The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers — [1] "first released in 1994" and "EMI America record label"

Ambiguities:
- "The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers" is "also titled Greatest Hits on some versions" ([1]) — title variants exist.
- User's word "album" could mean studio album vs. compilation; passage [3] labels Sock-Cess a "compilation album" and a "promotional only release."

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages provide the needed relation (name of the 1989 EMI-released album) and identify it as "Sock-Cess" ([3]); no additional facts are required to answer the question.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Sock-Cess" (Red Hot Chili Peppers album) or search "Sock-Cess Red Hot Chili Peppers 1989 EMI"
- Open "Red Hot Chili Peppers discography"
- Open page "Mother's Milk (album)" or search "Mother's Milk 1989 Red Hot Chili Peppers" to confirm studio-release distinctions
Final answer (resolved from passages): Sock-Cess.
response.hop2_query: Sock-Cess Red Hot Chili Peppers album EMI 1989
hop2_docs: Sock-Cess | Sock-Cess is a 1989 compilation album by Red Hot Chili Peppers and was released exclusively in the UK by EMI as a promotional only release.  The album contains tracks from the band's first four studio albums.

The Red Hot Chili Peppers (album) | The Red Hot Chili Peppers is the debut studio album by American rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers, released on August 10, 1984 on EMI Records.  The album was produced by Gang of Four guitarist Andy Gill, and is the only album to feature Jack Sherman on guitar.  Sherman was fired by the band after the album release tour, replaced by founding member Hillel Slovak. " Get Up and Jump" was the only single released from the album, but a music video was made for the song "True Men Don't Kill Coyotes".

The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers | The Best of Red Hot Chili Peppers (also titled Greatest Hits on some versions) is a compilation album by the Red Hot Chili Peppers, first released in 1994, containing tracks from the band's time signed to the EMI America record label. " What Hits!? " has all these songs and 8 more.  This compilation has been reprinted at least 4 times from 1994 to 2005 with different cover art.

Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers | Under the Covers: Essential Red Hot Chili Peppers is a compilation album released on March 31, 1998 by the Red Hot Chili Peppers.  All of the songs featured are covers mainly from the band's early albums.

The Abbey Road E.P. | The Abbey Road E.P. is an EP by The Red Hot Chili Peppers, released in the UK in May 1988 through EMI America as a way of introducing UK fans to the band's back catalog as they were touring the UK that year.  Out of the five tracks included on this EP, four had already been previously released on the band's studio albums, although the band's first two albums The Red Hot Chili Peppers and Freaky Styley were not released in the UK until 1990, thus it provided a way of introducing tracks to new listeners.
Can't Stop (Red Hot Chili Peppers song) | "Can't Stop" is a song by the Red Hot Chili Peppers from their 2002 album, "By the Way".  It is the third single released from the album.

ITunes Originals – Red Hot Chili Peppers | iTunes Originals – Red Hot Chili Peppers is a virtual album by Red Hot Chili Peppers which was released only on the iTunes Store as part of the "iTunes Originals" series of digital albums.  It features live versions of the band playing some of their more famous songs as well as featuring the band themselves talking about their careers.  It comprises videos of their live performances for the iTunes Originals as well as interviews about their careers, featuring the members of the band and Rick Rubin.  It is a video album.

Can't Stop (Red Hot Chili Peppers song)
ITunes Originals – Red Hot Chili Peppers
response.summary_2: Red Hot Chili Peppers' 1989 EMI release was Sock-Cess, a 1989 compilation album released exclusively in the UK by EMI as a promotional-only release.
